steps.title: Beyond Surface-Level
steps.body:
Most couples talk about logistics. Who's picking up the kids. What's for dinner. Whether you need to call the plumber.

That's not connection. That's coordination.

Emotional intimacy is the willingness to share your inner world — the messy, unpolished parts you usually keep to yourself.

It's saying:
• 'I'm actually really anxious about the presentation tomorrow.'
• 'I felt hurt when you didn't notice I cleaned the whole kitchen.'
• 'I'm scared we're drifting apart and I don't know what to do about it.'

Emotional intimacy is risky. That's exactly why it creates connection.

Vulnerability is not weakness.

It's the courage to say:
'I need you to see the real me —
not the put-together version,
not the one who has it all figured out.
The one who's scared, hopeful, confused, and wants you close.'

questions.label:
On a scale of 1–5, how comfortable are you being vulnerable with your partner?

Think about:
• Do you share when you're struggling — or do you wait until you've figured it out?
• Do you tell them when you're disappointed, or do you 'protect' them by staying quiet?
• Do you let them see you at your worst, or do you keep a polished front?

What would it take to share something vulnerable today?

steps.title: The Vulnerability Ladder
steps.body:
Vulnerability isn't all-or-nothing. It's a ladder you can climb step by step.

Rung 1 — Sharing facts: 'I had a rough day.'
Rung 2 — Sharing emotions: 'I felt really frustrated today.'
Rung 3 — Sharing needs: 'I needed someone to listen, and I didn't know how to ask.'
Rung 4 — Sharing fears: 'I'm worried I'm not good enough at my job.'
Rung 5 — Sharing relational fears: 'I'm scared that if you see this side of me, you'll think less of me.'

Most couples stay on rungs 1–2 their whole marriage. The deepest connections happen on rungs 3–5.

You don't need to go from 1 to 5 today. Just try one rung higher than usual.

steps.question: Emotional intimacy is built by:
options: Spending as much time together as possible
Sharing your inner world — feelings, fears, needs — with each other
feedback: Quantity of time helps, but it's about quality of sharing. Two quiet people in the same room isn't intimacy.
Yes. Emotional intimacy is built when both partners share what's actually going on inside them, not just what's happening around them.
Disagreement is healthy. Emotional intimacy is what happens when you can disagree and still feel close.
Physical chemistry is great, but without emotional intimacy, it doesn't sustain connection.

steps.title: Surface vs Deeper
steps.body:
Surface: 'Work was rough today.'

Deeper: 'I had a moment today where I felt like I don't know what I'm doing, and it shook me more than I expected.'

Surface: 'Sorry I was grumpy earlier.'

Deeper: 'I was grumpy earlier and I think it's because I felt invisible. I wanted you to notice me and I didn't know how to say it, so I just got quiet.'

The deeper versions are scarier to say. That's exactly why they build connection.

steps.title: Share Something Real
steps.prompt:
Take one step up the vulnerability ladder today.

Tell your partner something you'd normally keep to yourself. Something small is fine — just one rung higher than you usually go.

Template: 'I want to share something with you. I'm feeling [emotion] about [situation]. I'm telling you because I want you to know what's going on with me.'

Write what you'll share:
steps.body:
Take one step up the vulnerability ladder today.

Tell your partner something you'd normally keep to yourself. Something small is fine — just one rung higher than you usually go.

Template: 'I want to share something with you. I'm feeling [emotion] about [situation]. I'm telling you because I want you to know what's going on with me.'

Write what you'll share:

questions.label:
When your partner shares something vulnerable, how do you respond?

Do you:
• Jump to problem-solving? ('You should do X.')
• Minimize? ('It's not that bad.')
• Compare? ('Well, I had a worse day.')
• Or receive it? ('Thank you for telling me. I'm here.')

The way you receive their vulnerability determines whether they'll offer it again.

steps.question: When your partner shares a vulnerable feeling, the best response is to help them solve the problem.
feedback: Correct. Most people want to be heard before they want to be fixed. When someone shares vulnerability, the first response should be connection: 'I hear you. I'm with you.' Problem-solving can come later, and only if they ask.
Not quite. Most people actually need to be heard first. Problem-solving before connection can feel like dismissal. Try listening first, fixing later.

steps.title: Tomorrow: Play and Lightness
steps.body:
Emotional intimacy doesn't have to be heavy. In fact, some of the deepest connection happens through play, laughter, and joy.

Tomorrow: how to bring fun, spontaneity, and lightness back into your marriage — even in the middle of real life.

steps.title: Day Complete
steps.body: Great work today! You're building something real.
